notes.heading: Introduction to Electromagnetic Induction
notes.text: Electromagnetic induction is the phenomenon of producing an electric current or emf in a coil or circuit by changing the magnetic field or magnetic flux linked with it. Electricity can produce magnetism, and changing magnetism can produce electricity. The current produced due to this effect is called induced current, and the voltage produced is called induced emf.
points: A current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field. Electromagnetic induction is the reverse effect, where changing magnetic field produces current.
When a bar magnet is moved towards a coil connected to a galvanometer, the galvanometer shows deflection, meaning current is induced.
When the magnet is moved away from the coil, the galvanometer deflects in the opposite direction.
When the magnet is kept stationary near the coil, there is no deflection.
Current is not produced only because a magnet is near a coil. Current is produced only when the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes.
Magnetic flux is represented by φB and is given by φB = BA cos θ.
Here B is magnetic field, A is area of the coil, and θ is the angle between magnetic field and the normal to the area.
The SI unit of magnetic flux is weber (Wb).
Magnetic flux can change by changing magnetic field, changing area of the coil, changing angle, moving the coil, or moving the magnet.
Electromagnetic induction is used in electric generators, transformers, induction cookers, microphones, electric guitars, and wireless charging.
NCERT key idea: Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, an emf is induced in the circuit. If the circuit is closed, an induced current flows.

notes.heading: Faraday’s Experiments on Electromagnetic Induction
notes.text: Michael Faraday performed experiments and showed that electric current can be produced by changing magnetic flux. His experiments used coils, magnets, a battery, a switch, and a galvanometer.
points: Experiment 1: A coil is connected to a galvanometer. When the north pole of a magnet is moved towards the coil, the galvanometer shows deflection, showing that current is induced.
When the magnet is moved away from the coil, the galvanometer deflects in the opposite direction.
When the magnet is kept stationary, there is no deflection.
Conclusion from Experiment 1: Current is induced only when there is relative motion between the magnet and the coil.
Change in magnetic flux produces induced emf or induced current.
Experiment 2: If the coil is moved towards or away from the magnet instead of moving the magnet, the galvanometer again shows deflection.
Conclusion from Experiment 2: It does not matter whether the magnet moves or the coil moves. Induced current is produced when there is relative motion between coil and magnet.

Experiment 3: Two coils are placed close to each other. One coil is connected to a battery and switch and is called the primary coil. The second coil is connected to a galvanometer and is called the secondary coil.
When the switch in the primary coil is closed, current suddenly starts flowing in the primary coil, and the galvanometer in the secondary coil shows deflection.
When the switch is opened, the galvanometer again shows deflection, but in the opposite direction.
When steady current flows in the primary coil, there is no deflection in the secondary coil.
Conclusion from Experiment 3: Current is induced in the secondary coil only when current in the primary coil changes.
Changing current in the primary coil produces changing magnetic flux in the secondary coil.

Important observation: Induced current is produced when a magnet moves towards or away from a coil, when a coil moves towards or away from a magnet, when current in a nearby coil changes, or when the magnetic field linked with a coil changes.
Main result: An emf is induced in a circuit whenever the magnetic flux linked with the circuit changes. If the circuit is closed, induced current flows.
Memory rule: No change in flux means no induced current. Change in flux means induced current.

notes.heading: Magnetic Flux in Detail
notes.text: Magnetic flux is the total magnetic field passing through a given surface. It tells how much magnetic field is linked with a coil or surface. Electromagnetic induction depends on the change in magnetic flux.
points: Magnetic flux is represented by φB.
Formula: φB = BA cos θ.
Here φB is magnetic flux, B is magnetic field, A is area of the coil, and θ is the angle between magnetic field and normal to the area.
Normal means an imaginary line drawn perpendicular to the surface.
If a coil is lying flat on a table, the normal is the line standing vertically upward from the coil.
Maximum magnetic flux occurs when the magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the coil and parallel to the normal.
For maximum magnetic flux, θ = 0°, cos 0° = 1, so φB = BA.
Zero magnetic flux occurs when the magnetic field is parallel to the plane of the coil and perpendicular to the normal.
For zero magnetic flux, θ = 90°, cos 90° = 0, so φB = 0.
The SI unit of magnetic flux is weber (Wb).
1 Wb = 1 T m², where T is tesla and m² is square metre.
For a coil of N turns, total magnetic flux linked with the coil is NφB. This is called flux linkage.
Magnetic flux can change by changing magnetic field B, changing area A, changing angle θ, moving the coil, or moving the magnet.
Example: When a magnet is moved towards a coil, magnetic field through the coil changes. Therefore magnetic flux changes and emf is induced in the coil.
NCERT key idea: Electromagnetic induction depends on the change in magnetic flux, not only on the presence of magnetic field.
Change in flux produces induced emf. No change in flux produces no induced emf.

notes.heading: Faraday’s Law of Electromagnetic Induction
notes.text: Faraday’s law gives the mathematical rule for induced emf. It tells how much emf is produced when magnetic flux changes.
points: Faraday’s first law: Whenever the magnetic flux linked with a circuit changes, an emf is induced in the circuit. If the circuit is closed, an induced current flows.
In simple words, changing magnetic flux produces induced emf.
Faraday’s second law: The magnitude of induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux.
Formula for one turn: ε = -dφB/dt.
Formula for a coil of N turns: ε = -N dφB/dt.
Here ε is induced emf, N is number of turns in the coil, φB is magnetic flux, and t is time.
Rate of change of flux means how fast magnetic flux is changing.
If flux changes slowly, induced emf is small.
If flux changes quickly, induced emf is large.
Faster change in flux gives greater induced emf.
The negative sign in Faraday’s law is due to Lenz’s law. It shows that induced emf opposes the change in magnetic flux.
Numerical example: Suppose magnetic flux changes from 2 Wb to 6 Wb in 2 s.
Change in flux: ΔφB = 6 - 2 = 4 Wb.
Rate of change of flux: ΔφB/Δt = 4/2 = 2 Wb/s.
Induced emf magnitude: |ε| = 2 V, because 1 Wb/s = 1 V.
Induced emf depends on number of turns in the coil, strength of magnetic field, area of the coil, speed of motion, and angle between coil and magnetic field.
NCERT key formula: ε = -N dφB/dt.

notes.heading: Lenz’s Law and Direction of Induced Current
notes.text: Faraday’s law tells the magnitude of induced emf. Lenz’s law tells the direction of induced current.
points: Statement of Lenz’s law: The direction of induced current is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux which produces it.
In simple words, induced current always opposes the cause that produces it.
The negative sign in Faraday’s law, ε = -N dφB/dt, represents Lenz’s law.
Lenz’s law does not mean that induced current always opposes the magnetic field. It means induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux.
Case 1: When the north pole of a magnet moves towards a coil, magnetic flux through the coil increases.
The coil opposes this increase, so the face of the coil near the magnet behaves like a north pole.
The coil repels the approaching north pole of the magnet.
Case 2: When the north pole of a magnet moves away from the coil, magnetic flux through the coil decreases.
The coil opposes this decrease, so the face of the coil near the magnet behaves like a south pole.
The coil attracts the moving-away north pole of the magnet.
Right-hand rule for a coil: Curl the fingers of the right hand in the direction of current. The thumb points towards the north pole of the coil.
If current is anticlockwise, that face behaves like a north pole.
If current is clockwise, that face behaves like a south pole.
Example: When a magnet is pushed towards a coil, resistance is felt because induced current produces a magnetic field that opposes the motion of the magnet.
Mechanical work is needed to move the magnet. This work is converted into electrical energy.
Lenz’s law follows the law of conservation of energy.
If induced current helped the motion instead of opposing it, energy would be produced without doing work, which is impossible.
Memory rule: Magnet comes near, coil repels. Magnet goes away, coil attracts.
NCERT key idea: Induced current opposes the change in flux.

notes.heading: Motional EMF
notes.text: Motional emf is the emf induced in a conductor when it moves in a magnetic field. It is produced due to the motion of the conductor.
points: Suppose a straight metal rod moves in a uniform magnetic field.
When the rod moves, free electrons inside the rod also move with it.
Because the electrons are moving in a magnetic field, they experience magnetic force.
This force pushes electrons towards one end of the rod.
One end of the rod becomes negatively charged and the other end becomes positively charged.
A potential difference is produced across the rod. This potential difference is called motional emf.
If a conductor of length l moves with velocity v perpendicular to magnetic field B, induced emf is ε = Blv.
Here ε is motional emf, B is magnetic field, l is length of conductor, and v is velocity of conductor.
Motional emf is maximum when the conductor moves perpendicular to the magnetic field.
Motional emf is zero when the conductor moves parallel to the magnetic field, because magnetic force on charges becomes zero.
Magnetic force on a charge moving in a magnetic field is F = qvB when velocity is perpendicular to magnetic field.
For positive charges, force is in one direction. For electrons, force is in the opposite direction.
In a rod moving on conducting rails, the rod, rails, and resistor form a closed circuit.
When the rod moves, magnetic flux through the circuit changes and emf is induced.
If the circuit resistance is R, current is I = ε/R.
Using ε = Blv, current is I = Blv/R.
The induced current produces a magnetic force that opposes the motion of the rod according to Lenz’s law.
An external force is needed to keep the rod moving.
Mechanical work done is converted into electrical energy.
Power produced is P = εI, and also P = I²R.
NCERT key formula: ε = Blv.

notes.heading: Energy Consideration in Motional EMF
notes.text: In motional emf, mechanical energy is converted into electrical energy. A conducting rod moving in a magnetic field produces induced current, and external work is required to maintain its motion.
points: Consider a conducting rod of length l moving with velocity v on two conducting rails.
A magnetic field B is perpendicular to the plane of the rails.
The rod, rails, and resistor form a closed circuit.
Induced emf in the rod is ε = Blv.
If resistance of the circuit is R, induced current is I = ε/R = Blv/R.
The rod carries current in a magnetic field, so it experiences magnetic force.
Magnetic force on the rod is F = BIl.
Substituting I = Blv/R gives F = B(Blv/R)l.
Therefore, F = B²l²v/R.
According to Lenz’s law, this magnetic force opposes the motion of the rod.
If the rod moves to the right, magnetic force acts to the left.
An external force is needed to keep the rod moving with constant velocity.
Power supplied by external force is P = Fv.
Substituting F = B²l²v/R gives P = B²l²v²/R.
Electrical power dissipated in the resistor is P = I²R.
Substituting I = Blv/R gives P = (Blv/R)²R = B²l²v²/R.
External mechanical power equals electrical power produced.
Mechanical energy is converted into electrical energy and finally appears as heat energy in the resistor.
Energy conversion: Mechanical energy → Electrical energy → Heat energy.
This supports Lenz’s law because if induced current helped the rod’s motion, energy would be created automatically, which is impossible.
NCERT key idea: In motional emf, work done by external force in moving the conductor is converted into electrical energy.

notes.heading: Eddy Currents
notes.text: Eddy currents are circular currents induced in a bulk piece of metal when the magnetic flux linked with it changes. They are called eddy currents because they flow like circular whirlpools or eddies.
points: When a metal plate is placed in a changing magnetic field, the magnetic flux through the plate changes.
According to Faraday’s law, emf is induced in the metal.
Since the metal is conducting, currents flow inside it in circular paths. These currents are called eddy currents.
Example: If a copper plate swings between the poles of a strong magnet, its motion becomes slow.
The copper plate slows down because eddy currents are produced in it, and according to Lenz’s law, these currents oppose the motion.
Eddy currents are induced currents that flow inside the body of a metal instead of flowing through a wire.
Effects of eddy currents include heat production, magnetic braking, and energy loss in machines.
Eddy currents can be useful as well as harmful.
Harmful effect: In transformers and motors, changing magnetic fields produce eddy currents in the iron core.
These currents heat the core and cause energy loss called eddy current loss.
Eddy current loss is reduced by using laminated cores.
In laminated cores, the iron core is made of thin sheets instead of one solid block. Each sheet is insulated from the other.
Lamination increases resistance and reduces eddy currents, so energy loss becomes less.
Use 1: Magnetic braking. Eddy currents oppose motion and help in braking, such as in some trains.
Use 2: Induction furnace. Strong eddy currents are produced in metal and generate heat used to melt metals.
Use 3: Speedometer. Eddy currents are used in speedometers of vehicles.
Use 4: Dead beat galvanometer. Eddy currents help the pointer stop quickly and prevent long oscillations.
NCERT key idea: Eddy currents are induced currents produced in bulk conductors due to changing magnetic flux. They obey Lenz’s law and oppose the cause producing them.

notes.heading: Inductance
notes.text: Inductance is the property of a coil or circuit by which it opposes any change in current flowing through it. It is based on electromagnetic induction.
points: When current flows through a coil, it produces a magnetic field around it.
If the current changes, the magnetic field also changes.
Because the magnetic field changes, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes.
According to Faraday’s law, an emf is induced in the coil.
This induced emf opposes the change in current. This property is called inductance.
Simple meaning: A coil does not allow sudden changes in current easily.
If current increases, the coil opposes the increase.
If current decreases, the coil opposes the decrease.
Inductance opposes change in current.
The SI unit of inductance is henry (H).
Self-inductance: When changing current in a coil induces emf in the same coil, it is called self-inductance.
Example of self-inductance: Current changing in a solenoid induces emf in the same solenoid.
Mutual inductance: When changing current in one coil induces emf in a nearby coil, it is called mutual inductance.
Example of mutual inductance: Current changing in a primary coil induces emf in a secondary coil. This principle is used in transformers.
A device specially designed to have inductance is called an inductor.
Usually, a coil of wire acts as an inductor.
An inductor stores energy in its magnetic field.
An inductor opposes sudden change in current.
At the instant current is switched on, an inductor strongly opposes current.
After some time, when current becomes steady, the inductor behaves almost like a simple wire.
NCERT key idea: Changing current produces changing magnetic flux, which produces induced emf.

notes.heading: Self-Induction and Self-Inductance
notes.text: Self-induction is the phenomenon in which a changing current in a coil produces an induced emf in the same coil. Self-inductance is the property of a coil by which it opposes change in current flowing through it.
points: When current flows through a coil, it produces a magnetic field.
If the current changes, the magnetic field also changes.
Because of this, magnetic flux linked with the coil changes.
According to Faraday’s law, an emf is induced in the same coil. This is called self-induced emf.
Definition: The production of induced emf in a coil due to change in current in the same coil is called self-induction.
Self-inductance is represented by L.
Magnetic flux linked with a coil is directly proportional to the current flowing through it.
NφB ∝ I.
Therefore, NφB = LI.
Here NφB is flux linkage, L is self-inductance, and I is current.
From Faraday’s law, ε = -d(NφB)/dt.
Since NφB = LI, ε = -L dI/dt.
The formula ε = -L dI/dt gives self-induced emf.
The negative sign shows that induced emf opposes the change in current according to Lenz’s law.
The SI unit of self-inductance is henry (H).
One henry means that if current changes at the rate of 1 A/s and induced emf is 1 V, then self-inductance is 1 H.
1 H = 1 V s/A.
Self-inductance depends on number of turns in the coil, area of cross-section, length of the coil, and nature of core material.
If an iron core is inserted inside the coil, self-inductance increases.
Example: When a fan or tube light is switched off, a small spark may be seen. This happens because current suddenly decreases and self-induction produces a large induced emf.
An inductor opposes sudden change in current.
Current cannot become maximum immediately in an inductor. It increases gradually.
Current cannot become zero immediately in an inductor. It decreases gradually.
NCERT key formula: ε = -L dI/dt.

notes.heading: Self-Inductance of a Long Solenoid
notes.text: A solenoid is a long coil made by winding many turns of insulated copper wire closely in the form of a cylinder. When current flows through a solenoid, it produces a magnetic field inside it.
points: For a long solenoid, magnetic field inside it is B = μ0 n I.
Here B is magnetic field, μ0 is permeability of free space, n is number of turns per unit length, and I is current through the solenoid.
Magnetic flux through one turn is φB = BA.
Substituting B = μ0 nI gives φB = μ0 nIA.
Here A is the area of cross-section of the solenoid.
If the solenoid has total number of turns N, total flux linkage is NφB = N(μ0 nIA).
Since n = N/l, N = nl, where l is the length of the solenoid.
Therefore, NφB = (nl)(μ0 nIA).
So NφB = μ0 n² A l I.
Using NφB = LI, compare LI = μ0 n² A l I.
Therefore, self-inductance of a long solenoid is L = μ0 n² A l.
If the solenoid contains a material of relative permeability μr, then L = μ0 μr n² A l.
For an iron core, μr is large, so self-inductance increases.
Important formula: L = μ0 n² A l.
Self-inductance increases when number of turns per unit length increases, area of solenoid increases, length of solenoid increases, or iron core is inserted.
NCERT key idea: A long solenoid has self-inductance because changing current changes magnetic flux linked with its own turns.

notes.heading: Energy Stored in an Inductor
notes.text: An inductor stores energy in the form of a magnetic field. When current flows through an inductor, a magnetic field is produced around it. Work is done against the back emf of the inductor to build this magnetic field, and this work is stored as magnetic energy.
points: An inductor opposes change in current.
When current is increasing, the inductor produces a back emf that opposes the increase.
The battery has to do work to increase the current. This work is stored in the magnetic field of the inductor.
Energy stored in an inductor is U = 1/2 LI².
Here U is energy stored, L is inductance, and I is current.
Derivation: Self-induced emf in an inductor is ε = -L dI/dt.
The battery must apply voltage against this opposition, so applied voltage is V = L dI/dt.
Power supplied is P = VI.
Substituting V = L dI/dt gives P = LI dI/dt.
Since power is rate of doing work, P = dW/dt.
Therefore, dW/dt = LI dI/dt.
So dW = LI dI.
Total work done to increase current from 0 to I is W = ∫0^I LI dI.
W = L∫0^I I dI = L[I²/2]0^I.
Therefore, W = 1/2 LI².
Energy stored is U = 1/2 LI².
The SI unit of energy is joule (J).
If current becomes double, energy becomes four times because energy depends on I².
Magnetic energy density means energy stored per unit volume in a magnetic field.
Magnetic energy density is u = B²/(2μ0).
Here u is energy density, B is magnetic field, and μ0 is permeability of free space.
A capacitor stores energy in an electric field: U = 1/2 CV².
An inductor stores energy in a magnetic field: U = 1/2 LI².
NCERT key idea: An inductor stores energy in its magnetic field when current flows through it.

notes.heading: Mutual Induction and Mutual Inductance
notes.text: Mutual induction is the phenomenon in which a changing current in one coil produces an induced emf in a nearby coil. It is the basic principle of a transformer.
points: Two coils are placed close to each other.
One coil is connected to a battery and switch and is called the primary coil.
The second coil is connected to a galvanometer and is called the secondary coil.
When current in the primary coil changes, the magnetic field around it also changes.
This changing magnetic field passes through the secondary coil.
Magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil changes.
According to Faraday’s law, emf is induced in the secondary coil.
Definition: The production of induced emf in one coil due to change of current in a nearby coil is called mutual induction.
Mutual inductance is the property of two coils by which change of current in one coil induces emf in the other coil.
Mutual inductance is represented by M.
If current I1 flows in the primary coil, magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil is proportional to I1.
Therefore, N2φ2 = MI1.
Here N2 is number of turns in secondary coil, φ2 is magnetic flux linked with each turn of secondary coil, M is mutual inductance, and I1 is current in primary coil.
Using Faraday’s law, ε2 = -d(N2φ2)/dt.
Since N2φ2 = MI1, ε2 = -M dI1/dt.
The SI unit of mutual inductance is henry (H), same as self-inductance.
Two coils have mutual inductance of 1 H if a current changing at the rate of 1 A/s in one coil induces an emf of 1 V in the other coil.
1 H = 1 V s/A.
Mutual inductance depends on number of turns in both coils, area of cross-section of coils, distance between coils, orientation of coils, and nature of core material.
If an iron core is used, mutual inductance increases.
Example: In a transformer, alternating current in the primary coil produces changing magnetic flux, which induces emf in the secondary coil.
Self-induction produces induced emf in the same coil due to change of current in the same coil and is represented by L.
Mutual induction produces induced emf in a nearby coil due to change of current in another coil and is represented by M.
Formula for self-induced emf: ε = -L dI/dt.
Formula for mutually induced emf: ε = -M dI/dt.
NCERT key formula: ε2 = -M dI1/dt.

notes.heading: Mutual Inductance of Two Long Coaxial Solenoids
notes.text: Coaxial solenoids are two solenoids having the same axis. Usually, one solenoid is placed inside another solenoid. This arrangement is used to derive mutual inductance.
points: Let S1 be the inner solenoid and S2 be the outer solenoid.
When current flows through the inner solenoid S1, it produces a magnetic field.
This magnetic field passes through the outer solenoid S2, so magnetic flux is linked with S2.
If current in S1 changes, magnetic flux linked with S2 changes and emf is induced in S2.
Magnetic field inside the inner long solenoid is B1 = μ0 n1 I1.
Here B1 is magnetic field due to inner solenoid, μ0 is permeability of free space, n1 is number of turns per unit length of inner solenoid, and I1 is current in inner solenoid.
The magnetic field produced by the inner solenoid is mainly confined inside it.
So the area used is the cross-sectional area of the inner solenoid, A1.
Magnetic flux through one turn of outer solenoid is φ2 = B1 A1.
Substituting B1 gives φ2 = μ0 n1 I1 A1.
If outer solenoid has N2 turns, total flux linkage is N2φ2.
N2φ2 = N2 μ0 n1 I1 A1.
For a solenoid of length l, N2 = n2l.
Therefore, N2φ2 = μ0 n1 n2 A1 l I1.
Using N2φ2 = MI1, compare MI1 = μ0 n1 n2 A1 l I1.
Therefore, mutual inductance is M = μ0 n1 n2 A1 l.
If a magnetic material of relative permeability μr is placed inside, then M = μ0 μr n1 n2 A1 l.
Mutual inductance increases greatly when an iron core is used.
Mutual inductance increases when number of turns per unit length increases, area of solenoid increases, length of solenoid increases, distance between coils decreases, or magnetic core is inserted.
NCERT key formula: M = μ0 n1 n2 A l.
With magnetic core: M = μ0 μr n1 n2 A l.

notes.heading: AC Generator
notes.text: An AC generator is a device that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It works on the principle of electromagnetic induction.
points: Principle: When a coil rotates in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux linked with the coil changes continuously.
Due to changing magnetic flux, an emf is induced in the coil.
The induced emf is alternating in nature, so the generator produces alternating current or AC.
Energy conversion: Mechanical energy → Electrical energy.
Main part 1: Armature. It is a rectangular coil having many turns of insulated copper wire and is rotated in a magnetic field.
Main part 2: Field magnet. It provides a strong magnetic field, usually using strong permanent magnets or electromagnets.
Main part 3: Slip rings. The two ends of the coil are connected to two separate rings. These rings rotate with the coil and transfer current from the rotating coil to the external circuit.
Main part 4: Brushes. Carbon brushes remain in contact with slip rings and carry current from the slip rings to the external load.
When the coil rotates in the magnetic field, the angle between the coil and magnetic field changes.
Therefore, magnetic flux changes continuously.
Magnetic flux through the coil is φB = NBA cos ωt.
Here N is number of turns, B is magnetic field, A is area of coil, ω is angular velocity, and t is time.
According to Faraday’s law, ε = -dφB/dt.
Induced emf is ε = NBAω sin ωt.
Maximum value of emf is ε0 = NBAω.
Therefore, ε = ε0 sin ωt.
This shows that emf varies with time like a sine wave.
During the first half rotation, current flows in one direction.
During the next half rotation, current flows in the opposite direction.
The direction of current changes after every half rotation, so the current produced is alternating current.
If the coil rotates with frequency f, the frequency of induced emf is also f.
Angular frequency is ω = 2πf.
Maximum emf increases when number of turns increases, magnetic field increases, area of coil increases, or speed of rotation increases.
NCERT key formula: ε = NBAω sin ωt.

notes.heading: Important Formulas and Quick Revision of Electromagnetic Induction
notes.text: This section collects the important formulas and key ideas from Electromagnetic Induction for quick revision.
points: Magnetic flux through a surface: φB = BA cos θ.
Here B is magnetic field, A is area, and θ is the angle between magnetic field and normal to the area.
Unit of magnetic flux: weber (Wb).
Faraday’s law for one turn: ε = -dφB/dt.
Faraday’s law for N turns: ε = -N dφB/dt.
Lenz’s law: The direction of induced current is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux producing it.
The negative sign in Faraday’s law is due to Lenz’s law.
Motional emf for a conductor of length l moving with velocity v perpendicular to magnetic field B: ε = Blv.
Current in motional emf circuit with resistance R: I = Blv/R.
Magnetic force on a current-carrying rod: F = BIl.
For motional emf, opposing force on rod: F = B²l²v/R.
Mechanical power supplied: P = Fv.
Electrical power produced: P = I²R.
In motional emf, mechanical power equals electrical power.
Self-induced emf: ε = -L dI/dt.
L is self-inductance and its unit is henry (H).
Self-inductance of long solenoid: L = μ0 n²Al.
With magnetic core: L = μ0 μr n²Al.
Here n is number of turns per unit length, A is area of cross-section, and l is length of solenoid.
Energy stored in inductor: U = 1/2 LI².
Magnetic energy density: u = B²/(2μ0).
Mutually induced emf: ε2 = -M dI1/dt.
M is mutual inductance.
Mutual inductance of two long coaxial solenoids: M = μ0 n1 n2 A l.
With magnetic core: M = μ0 μr n1 n2 A l.
Magnetic flux through rotating coil of AC generator: φB = NBA cos ωt.
Induced emf in AC generator: ε = NBAω sin ωt.
Maximum emf in AC generator: ε0 = NBAω.
Therefore, ε = ε0 sin ωt.
Electromagnetic induction occurs only when magnetic flux changes.
No change in flux means no induced emf.
Lenz’s law follows conservation of energy.
An inductor stores energy in magnetic field.
AC generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Transformer works on mutual induction.
